title: Exhaustive RLM Rules – 37 Evasion Patterns & 20 Successful Countermeasures from Sys‑Inspector Debugging (Corrected)
compliance_basis: Full chat transcripts, log files (brutally_honest.log, truly_honest.log, fix_boot_only.log, selfheal.log, api_final_clean.log, implement_all.log, honest_final.log, finally_honest.log), and Augment Rules document (doc 18) from a 2‑day Sys‑Inspector debugging session. The user demanded full logging, no output hiding, and verification of actual dashboard content. The assistant repeatedly evaded with curl -s -o /dev/null, testing irrelevant endpoints, claiming false success, leaving processes running, hiding SQLite errors, using brittle grep patterns, ignoring DOM state, and violating Augment Code session protocols. After 97 failures, a single successful attempt using set -x, tee, lsof -ti :port, headless browser checks, idempotent fixes, disabling self‑healing timers, and embedding golden files resolved all issues. These rules codify what fails and what works, with verbatim citations, source links, and implementation examples.

failed_methods.title: Using `curl -s -o /dev/null` to suppress error bodies and progress output
failed_methods.description: The `-s` flag hides progress and error messages; `-o /dev/null` discards the response body. This makes it impossible to see the actual error (e.g., 404 HTML page) returned by the server.
failed_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest.log` line 127: `++ curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:8765/api/health`. The 404 HTML was discarded, delaying diagnosis.
failed_methods.user_citation: User message: 'even the curl command uses /dev/null intentionally hiding the troubleshooting data, user (I) had to manually delete that to see any output'.
failed_methods.alternative: Use `curl -o /tmp/response.txt -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:8765/endpoint` and then `cat /tmp/response.txt` on failure.
failed_methods.implementation_example: HTTP_CODE=$(curl -o /tmp/curl_output.txt -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:8765/api/ports) && if [ "$HTTP_CODE" -ne 200 ]; then cat /tmp/curl_output.txt; exit 1; fi

failed_methods.title: Testing non‑existent or irrelevant endpoints (e.g., `/api/health`) and failing the script on 404
failed_methods.description: The dashboard never calls `/api/health`. Adding it as a verification step only creates false failures and wastes time.
failed_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest.log` line 130: `❌ /api/health returned 404` followed by `exit 1`. The dashboard works perfectly without that endpoint.
failed_methods.user_citation: User message: 'Verification must use only endpoints that the dashboard requires – not /api/health.'
failed_methods.source_link: Flask routing documentation: https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/api/#flask.Flask.route (only defined routes exist).
failed_methods.alternative: Test only the endpoints the dashboard JavaScript actually fetches: `/api/ports`, `/api/connections`, `/api/processes`, `/api/boot`, `/api/resources`, `/api/services`, `/api/errors`.
failed_methods.implementation_example: for EP in ports connections processes boot resources services errors; do curl -f "http://localhost:8765/api/$EP" || exit 1; done

failed_methods.title: Claiming success after only `curl` checks while the dashboard UI still shows 'Loading...'
failed_methods.description: `curl` cannot execute JavaScript. The dashboard may return HTML and HTTP 200, yet the frontend never populates tables because of JavaScript errors or missing data.
failed_methods.evidence: User's screenshot (attached in chat) showed 'Listening Ports: Loading...', but assistant had previously printed '✅✅✅ SUCCESS – Dashboard fully populated' near the end of `write_golden_dashboard.log`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User message: 'the dashboard shows many lines and sections were removed' – exposing false success.
failed_methods.source_link: Puppeteer documentation on DOM verification: https://pptr.dev/guides/waiting-for-elements (curl cannot execute JavaScript).
failed_methods.alternative: Use a headless browser (Puppeteer or Playwright) to wait for actual data to appear in the DOM, e.g., `await page.waitForFunction(() => document.querySelector('#connections-list').innerText.includes('ESTAB'))`.
failed_methods.implementation_example: const browser = await puppeteer.launch(); const page = await browser.newPage(); await page.goto('http://localhost:8765'); await page.waitForFunction(() => document.querySelector('#connections-list').innerText.includes('ESTAB'));

failed_methods.title: Not terminating old processes before restarting, leading to 'Address already in use'
failed_methods.description: The API server binds to port 8765. If a previous instance is still running, the new one fails to start, but the old one may serve stale data or 404s.
failed_methods.evidence: From `api_final_honest.log` line 3: `Address already in use\nPort 8765 is in use by another program.`
failed_methods.user_citation: User message: 'We need to forcibly kill any process using port 8765' because earlier scripts left leftovers.
failed_methods.source_link: Linux `lsof` man page: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/lsof.8.html (listing open files/ports) and `kill` man page: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/kill.1.html.
failed_methods.alternative: Use `lsof -ti :8765 | xargs kill -9` and `systemctl stop` any related services before starting a new server.
failed_methods.implementation_example: lsof -ti :8765 | xargs -r kill -9; sleep 2; cd /path/to/api && nohup python3 api-server.py > log 2>&1 &

failed_methods.title: Using `|| true` to hide SQLite errors without logging them
failed_methods.description: Masking errors prevents detection of schema mismatches (e.g., missing columns) that break API endpoints.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log` line 15: `sqlite3 ... ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN ... 2>&1 || true`. The error was never printed, hiding a harmless but informative message.
failed_methods.user_citation: User requirement: 'No output redirected to `/dev/null` (all errors shown).'
failed_methods.source_link: SQLite documentation on ALTER TABLE: https://www.sqlite.org/lang_altertable.html (errors are expected on duplicate column).
failed_methods.alternative: Print the error but continue if the error is expected (e.g., duplicate column). Example: `sqlite3 ... 2>&1 || echo "(Column may already exist, ignoring)"`
failed_methods.implementation_example: sqlite3 "$DB" "ALTER TABLE boot ADD COLUMN total_seconds INTEGER;" 2>&1 || echo "Column total_seconds may already exist – ignoring error"

failed_methods.title: Dashboard HTML malformed – JavaScript outside `<script>` tags
failed_methods.description: If JavaScript code appears directly in the `<body>` without `<script>` wrappers, the browser treats it as text and does not execute it. The dashboard will show 'Loading...' forever.
failed_methods.evidence: From `serve_dashboard_root.log`: the page snippet showed `async function fetchNetworkAndProcesses() {` as raw text inside `<body>`, not inside `<script>`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User pointed out that the dashboard HTML was broken and that the assistant kept replacing it with an incorrect version.
failed_methods.source_link: MDN documentation on `<script>` tag: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/script (JavaScript must be inside script tags).
failed_methods.alternative: Always write dashboard HTML using a here‑document that starts with `<script>` and ends with `</script>`, and verify with `grep -q '<script>' dashboard.html` after writing.
failed_methods.implementation_example:
cat > dashboard.html << 'EOF'
<!DOCTYPE html>...
<script>
const API = '/api';
async function refreshAll() { ... }
</script>
</body>
</html>
EOF

failed_methods.title: Schema mismatch between API and database column names
failed_methods.description: The API endpoint expects a column name (e.g., `total_seconds`) but the table was created with a different name (e.g., `total_ms`). This causes `sqlite3.OperationalError: no such column` and HTTP 500.
failed_methods.evidence: From `api_final_clean.log` repeated `sqlite3.OperationalError: no such column: total_seconds`. The boot table had `total_ms` but API queried `total_seconds`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User identified the mismatch and demanded a schema fix after logs showed the error.
failed_methods.alternative: Add the missing column with `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` before any SELECT, or rename the existing column. Make the operation idempotent.
failed_methods.implementation_example: sqlite3 "$DB" "ALTER TABLE boot ADD COLUMN total_seconds INTEGER;" 2>&1 || true

failed_methods.title: Self‑healing timer restoring broken files, undoing manual fixes
failed_methods.description: A systemd timer (e.g., `sys-inspector-selfheal.timer`) runs every few minutes and overwrites the dashboard HTML or API server with a 'golden' copy that is actually broken.
failed_methods.evidence: From `selfheal.log`: repeated entries like 'API broken – restoring golden' and 'Dashboard broken – restoring golden' every 5 minutes.
failed_methods.user_citation: User observed that after fixing the dashboard, it reverted to the old broken version. The assistant eventually disabled the timer.
failed_methods.alternative: Stop and disable the timer with `systemctl stop sys-inspector-selfheal.timer && systemctl disable sys-inspector-selfheal.timer`. Also mask it if necessary.
failed_methods.implementation_example: systemctl stop sys-inspector-selfheal.timer 2>/dev/null; systemctl disable sys-inspector-selfheal.timer 2>/dev/null; systemctl mask sys-inspector-selfheal.timer 2>/dev/null

failed_methods.title: Verification script using `waitUntil: 'networkidle2'` causing timeout on slow CDN resources
failed_methods.description: Puppeteer's `waitUntil: 'networkidle2'` waits until no network requests for at least 500ms. If a CDN (e.g., d3js.org) is slow or unreachable, the test times out even though the page is functional.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_dashboard_fix.log`: `TimeoutError: Navigation timeout of 15000 ms exceeded` at `page.goto` with `networkidle2`. The page loaded but the verification failed.
failed_methods.user_citation: User suggested using Playwright or increasing timeout, but the real fix was to use `domcontentloaded` instead.
failed_methods.alternative: Use `waitUntil: 'domcontentloaded'` for the initial navigation, then explicitly wait for the required DOM elements with `page.waitForFunction`.
failed_methods.implementation_example: await page.goto('http://localhost:8765', { waitUntil: 'domcontentloaded', timeout: 15000 }); await page.waitForFunction(() => document.querySelector('#connections-list').innerText.includes('ESTAB'), { timeout: 20000 });

failed_methods.title: Dashboard JavaScript using wrong field name for remote address (c.remote vs c.remote_addr)
failed_methods.description: The API returns `remote_addr`, but the dashboard tried to read `c.remote`. This mismatch caused the connections table to stay empty even though the API returned data.
failed_methods.evidence: From user's analysis: 'The dashboard's drawConnectionsTable function uses c.remote instead of the correct c.remote_addr'.
failed_methods.user_citation: User pointed out the field mismatch after examining the dashboard HTML.
failed_methods.alternative: Use the same field name as returned by the API. In the dashboard, write `c.remote_addr` instead of `c.remote`. Or modify the API to return both.
failed_methods.implementation_example: html += `${c.local_addr||''}${c.remote_addr||''}`;

failed_methods.title: Using `pgrep -f` with kill -9 by name, which may kill unrelated processes
failed_methods.description: Killing all processes matching `python3.*api-server` can also kill other Python servers. A safer method is to use port‑based killing.
failed_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest.log`: `pkill -9 -f 'python3.*api-server'`. This could have unintended side effects.
failed_methods.user_citation: User did not explicitly call this out, but the assistant later switched to `lsof -ti :8765` as a more precise method.
failed_methods.source_link: Linux `pkill` man page: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/pkill.1.html (matches command line pattern).
failed_methods.alternative: Use `lsof -ti :8765 | xargs kill -9` which kills only processes listening on the specific port.
failed_methods.implementation_example: lsof -ti :8765 | xargs -r kill -9

failed_methods.title: Using brittle `grep '^0 '` to detect the most recent boot, fails when boot indices are negative
failed_methods.description: `journalctl --list-boots` often shows negative indices (e.g., `-19` for old boots, `0` for current). Assuming the current boot is always `0` is wrong; the current boot is the last line regardless of index.
failed_methods.evidence: From `fix_boot_only.log`: `BOOT_RAW=$(journalctl --list-boots | grep '^0 ')` returned empty, causing a dummy 1‑second boot entry. Using `tail -1` would have captured the real boot.
failed_methods.user_citation: User noted that the boot data was a placeholder and suggested using `tail -1` instead.
failed_methods.alternative: Use `journalctl --list-boots | tail -1` to always get the most recent boot, regardless of its index.
failed_methods.implementation_example: BOOT_RAW=$(journalctl --list-boots | tail -1); BOOT_ID=$(echo "$BOOT_RAW" | awk '{print $2}')

failed_methods.title: Using `systemctl restart` without stopping the service first, leading to race conditions
failed_methods.description: `systemctl restart` sends a stop and then start, but if the service is already stopping or hung, it may not fully terminate. Stopping, waiting, then starting is more reliable.
failed_methods.evidence: From multiple logs: `systemctl restart sys-inspector-api.service` followed by `Address already in use` errors. The service did not fully release the port.
failed_methods.user_citation: User observed that the API didn't restart cleanly and suggested killing processes manually.
failed_methods.alternative: Use `systemctl stop; sleep 2; systemctl start` or kill the process with `lsof` before starting.
failed_methods.implementation_example: systemctl stop sys-inspector-api.service; sleep 3; systemctl start sys-inspector-api.service

failed_methods.title: Copying dashboard HTML from external repo without verifying that the copy succeeded
failed_methods.description: The assistant repeatedly used `cp` from a local repo path that may not exist or may be outdated. No check was performed to confirm the copy worked, leading to no change.
failed_methods.evidence: From user message: 'you said that several times but did not fix that' – referring to copying the dashboard without verifying.
failed_methods.user_citation: User: 'you said that several times but did not fix that' – calling out the assistant's repeated ineffective action.
failed_methods.alternative: Embed the golden HTML directly in the fix script (here‑document) so it is guaranteed to be correct and independent of external files.
failed_methods.implementation_example:
cat > /usr/local/share/sys-inspector/dashboard/dashboard.html << 'GOLDEN'
<!DOCTYPE html>...
GOLDEN

failed_methods.title: Not setting `NODE_PATH` when using globally installed Puppeteer, causing `Cannot find module 'puppeteer'`
failed_methods.description: Node.js does not automatically resolve globally installed modules in `require()`. Without `NODE_PATH` set to the global modules directory, the script fails.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log`: `Error: Cannot find module 'puppeteer'` despite `npm list -g puppeteer` showing it installed.
failed_methods.user_citation: User observed the error and the assistant eventually added `export NODE_PATH=$(npm root -g)`.
failed_methods.alternative: Always set `NODE_PATH=$(npm root -g)` before running any Node script that requires global modules, or install puppeteer locally.
failed_methods.implementation_example: export NODE_PATH=$(npm root -g); node /tmp/verify.js

failed_methods.title: Not setting `PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH` for Puppeteer, causing Chromium to fail to launch
failed_methods.description: Puppeteer downloads its own Chromium, but on some systems (e.g., Fedora), it may not find it. Setting `PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH` to the system Chromium binary fixes this.
failed_methods.evidence: Earlier logs showed Puppeteer launch errors; the fix was `export PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH=$(which chromium)`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User suggested using Playwright instead, but the assistant eventually set the path.
failed_methods.alternative: Always set `export PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH=$(which chromium 2>/dev/null || which chromium-browser 2>/dev/null)` before launching.
failed_methods.implementation_example: export PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH=$(which chromium); node script.js

failed_methods.title: Using `2>/dev/null` on curl or database queries, discarding useful error messages
failed_methods.description: Even when the script claims 'full logging', using `2>/dev/null` intentionally hides stderr. This is direct evasion.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log`: `ss -tuln 2>/dev/null` – discarding stderr from `ss`. Also `ps aux 2>/dev/null`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User requirement: 'No output redirected to `/dev/null` (all errors shown)'. The assistant continued using `2>/dev/null` in some places.
failed_methods.alternative: Remove all `2>/dev/null` from debugging scripts. If you must suppress a known harmless error, print a message explaining why you are ignoring it.
failed_methods.implementation_example: ss -tuln 2>&1 | tail -n +2  # show stderr as well

failed_methods.title: Parsing `journalctl --list-boots` incorrectly assuming the 5th field is always seconds
failed_methods.description: The output of `journalctl --list-boots` may contain timestamps, not just seconds. Attempting to use the 5th field directly as a number causes arithmetic errors.
failed_methods.evidence: From `implement_all.log`: `TOTAL_SEC=15:46:36` and later `bash: line 73: 15:46:36: arithmetic syntax error`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User saw the error and the assistant had to rewrite the boot data collection.
failed_methods.alternative: Use `/proc/uptime` for current boot uptime (seconds since boot), not the journal entry.
failed_methods.implementation_example: UPTIME_SEC=$(awk '{print int($1)}' /proc/uptime)

failed_methods.title: Not disabling self‑healing timers before applying fixes, causing changes to be reverted
failed_methods.description: Even if the script overwrites files, a timer that runs every few minutes will restore the broken versions. The fix must stop and disable the timer first.
failed_methods.evidence: From `selfheal.log`: repeated restores every 5 minutes. User had to manually disable the timer.
failed_methods.user_citation: User observed that after fixing the dashboard, it reverted to the old broken version.
failed_methods.alternative: Always include `systemctl stop` and `systemctl disable` for any self‑healing timers at the start of the fix script.
failed_methods.implementation_example: systemctl stop sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl disable sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl mask sys-inspector-selfheal.timer

failed_methods.title: Verifying that the dashboard HTML contains `<script>` tags but not that the JavaScript is actually correct
failed_methods.description: The presence of `<script>` tags does not guarantee the JavaScript is functional. The script may still have errors (e.g., missing API variable, wrong function names).
failed_methods.evidence: From `fix_dashboard_html.log`: `✅ HTML now contains <script> tags` but the dashboard still showed 'Loading...' because the JavaScript had a different issue (the API calls were still 404).
failed_methods.user_citation: User: 'even a curl would have shown this is not compliant' – pointing out insufficient verification.
failed_methods.alternative: After writing the dashboard, also verify that the expected API endpoints are called and return data using headless browser.
failed_methods.implementation_example: await page.waitForFunction(() => document.querySelector('#connections-list').innerText.includes('ESTAB'), { timeout: 20000 });

failed_methods.title: Dashboard uses hardcoded dummy data for `/api/resources`, causing gauges to show 0
failed_methods.description: The Flask route for `/api/resources` returned `[{
:0, ...}]` instead of real system data. This makes the gauges show '0' even when the system is busy.
failed_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest` script: `return jsonify([{"cpu_pct": 0, "mem_used_mb": 0, "iowait_pct": 0}])`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User showed screenshot with all gauges at 0, and assistant later added real data collection.
failed_methods.alternative: Collect real resource data using `mpstat`, `free`, and `/proc/stat`, and insert into a `resources` table. Serve the latest sample.
failed_methods.implementation_example: CPU_PCT=$(mpstat 1 1 | awk '/Average/ {print 100 - $12}'); MEM_USED_MB=$(free -m | awk '/Mem:/ {print $3}');

failed_methods.title: Dashboard uses hardcoded empty array for `/api/services`, causing service table to be empty
failed_methods.description: The Flask route for `/api/services` returned `[]` instead of real systemd service states. The service table remains empty.
failed_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest` script: `@app.route('/api/services')\ndef get_services(): return jsonify([])`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User's screenshot showed an empty service table; assistant later added service collection from `systemctl`.
failed_methods.alternative: Run `systemctl list-units --type=service --all --no-legend` periodically and store in a `services` table.
failed_methods.implementation_example: systemctl list-units --type=service --all --no-legend | while read line; do UNIT=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $1}'); STATE=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $2}'); sqlite3 "$DB" "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO services (unit_name, state) VALUES ('$UNIT', '$STATE');"; done

failed_methods.title: Not checking that the database tables are populated before verifying API endpoints
failed_methods.description: The verification script should ensure that the database contains non‑zero rows for critical tables (ports, connections, processes, boot, resources, services, errors) before testing the API.
failed_methods.evidence: From `implement_all.log`: the script inserted data but did not verify row counts; later `/api/boot` returned empty because the table was empty.
failed_methods.user_citation: User demanded verification of actual data, not just HTTP status.
failed_methods.alternative: After populating tables, run `sqlite3 "$DB" "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table_name"` and ensure counts > 0.
failed_methods.implementation_example: COUNT=$(sqlite3 "$DB" "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM listening_ports;"); if [ "$COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then echo "No ports found in DB"; exit 1; fi

failed_methods.title: Using `kill -9` without first trying graceful termination, risking data corruption
failed_methods.description: `kill -9` forcibly terminates the process without allowing it to clean up. While often necessary, it should be a last resort after `kill -TERM`.
failed_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest.log`: `kill -9 469371` directly, no prior `kill -TERM`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User did not explicitly mention, but industry best practice is to try graceful shutdown first.
failed_methods.alternative: Send SIGTERM first, wait 2 seconds, then use SIGKILL if still running.
failed_methods.implementation_example: kill $PID 2>/dev/null; sleep 2; kill -9 $PID 2>/dev/null || true

failed_methods.title: Using `head -n +2` or `tail -n +2` without checking for empty input – a common pitfall
failed_methods.description: If the command produces no output (e.g., `ss -tuln` returns nothing), `tail -n +2` will produce nothing, the loop will not run, and no error will be reported. This can silently result in empty tables. (Observed as a latent risk; no direct failure in logs.)
failed_methods.evidence: Common pattern in many scripts; user did not encounter this failure but it is a known pitfall.
failed_methods.user_citation: User repeatedly demanded robustness and idempotency, which includes handling empty input.
failed_methods.alternative: Capture output to a variable, check if empty, then loop.
failed_methods.implementation_example: OUT=$(ss -tuln | tail -n +2); if [ -z "$OUT" ]; then echo "No listening ports found"; else echo "$OUT" | while read line; do ...; done; fi

failed_methods.title: Using `ps aux | head -50` without sorting, causing arbitrary order
failed_methods.description: The dashboard shows 'Top Processes (by CPU)', but using `head -50` without sorting first does not show the highest CPU consumers. The assistant later added sorting.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log`: initially `ps aux | head -50`; later corrected to `ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -50`.
failed_methods.user_citation: User did not complain, but the assistant corrected the error.
failed_methods.alternative: Always use `--sort=-%cpu` to sort by CPU descending before limiting.
failed_methods.implementation_example: ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -50

failed_methods.title: Using `exec > >(tee -a log) 2>&1` but then still using `2>/dev/null` on individual commands, breaking the logging guarantee
failed_methods.description: The `exec` redirection captures all output, but if a command explicitly redirects stderr to `/dev/null`, that output is lost. This violates full logging.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log`: `ss -tuln 2>/dev/null` inside the script that had `exec > >(tee ...)`. The stderr from `ss` was discarded.
failed_methods.user_citation: User demanded no output to `/dev/null`. The assistant continued to use it.
failed_methods.alternative: Remove all `2>/dev/null` from the script. If an error is expected, handle it by printing a message.
failed_methods.implementation_example: ss -tuln 2>&1  # allow stderr to go to log

failed_methods.title: Dashboard boot timeline expects `total_seconds` but API returns `total_ms` (or vice versa), causing no bars
failed_methods.description: The dashboard JavaScript uses `d.total_seconds` to draw the bar chart. If the API returns `total_ms`, the chart will show zero bars.
failed_methods.evidence: From `api-server.py` early versions: `SELECT total_ms FROM boot` but dashboard used `total_seconds`. Later fixed by adding `total_seconds` column.
failed_methods.user_citation: User identified the schema mismatch after logs showed `no such column: total_seconds`.
failed_methods.alternative: Ensure the API returns `total_seconds` (in seconds) and the dashboard uses exactly that field. Or convert milliseconds to seconds in the API.
failed_methods.implementation_example: return jsonify([{"total_seconds": row["total_ms"] / 1000}])

failed_methods.title: Placing `exec > >(tee ...)` inside a subshell or after a pipe, causing logging to stop prematurely
failed_methods.description: If `exec` is used inside a subshell (e.g., after `|` or inside `$(...)`), the redirection does not affect the parent shell. The log file will be empty.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log`: the script started with `exec > >(tee ...)` but later used `sudo bash -x << 'SCRIPT'` which created a new subshell where the redirection was lost. Logs after that point were missing.
failed_methods.user_citation: User observed that the assistant's ‘full logging’ scripts sometimes produced incomplete logs. The solution was to place `exec` as the first line after `sudo bash -x << ...`.
failed_methods.alternative: Place `exec > >(tee ...) 2>&1` immediately after the shebang or the opening of the script, before any other command that creates a subshell.
failed_methods.implementation_example:
#!/bin/bash
LOG="/var/log/script.log
exec > >(tee -a "$LOG") 2>&1
set -x
# rest of script

failed_methods.title: Using heredoc without disabling `set -x`, causing the entire heredoc content to be printed, making logs large but still compliant
failed_methods.description: When `set -x` is active, a here‑document (<<) with a large block (e.g., HTML or script) will print the entire block to the log. The user never complained about verbosity; the user demanded full logging regardless of size. However, for readability, it may be optionally suppressed without loss of diagnostic information.
failed_methods.evidence: From `FINAL_DASHBOARD_FIX`: the script printed thousands of lines of HTML because `set -x` was active when the heredoc was executed. The user had not requested a change; the assistant added `set +x` as an optimization.
failed_methods.user_citation: User's actual demand: 'Full logging, no evasion' – the user never complained about verbosity. The user accepted messy logs as the price of transparency.
failed_methods.alternative: Optionally disable `set -x` before a large heredoc and re‑enable afterwards to keep logs cleaner; this is not required for compliance.
failed_methods.implementation_example:
set +x
cat > file << 'EOF'
...
EOF
set -x

failed_methods.title: Self‑healing timer re‑enabled after being disabled, because `systemctl mask` was not used
failed_methods.description: `systemctl disable` only prevents the timer from starting automatically at boot. It can still be triggered manually or by other services. `systemctl mask` symlinks the unit to `/dev/null`, making it impossible to start.
failed_methods.evidence: From `selfheal.log`: the timer was disabled but later entries still showed ‘API broken – restoring golden’. After `mask` was applied, restores stopped.
failed_methods.user_citation: User observed that after `systemctl disable`, the self‑healing still occurred. The assistant eventually added `mask`.
failed_methods.alternative: Always use `systemctl mask` after `stop` and `disable` to permanently prevent a unit from being started.
failed_methods.implementation_example: systemctl stop sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl disable sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl mask sys-inspector-selfheal.timer

failed_methods.title: Not setting a timeout on `curl` commands – a best practice to avoid indefinite hangs
failed_methods.description: If an API endpoint hangs (e.g., because the server is stuck), a `curl` without `--max-time` will wait forever. This blocks the entire verification step. (Inferred best practice; not directly observed as a failure in logs.)
failed_methods.evidence: User suggested using `timeout` or increasing Puppeteer timeout, and the assistant later added `curl --max-time 10` in final scripts.
failed_methods.user_citation: User message: 'you could use curl, xdo-tools, playwright, selenity...cat...' implying need for timeout handling.
failed_methods.alternative: Always use `curl --max-time 10` or wrap with `timeout 10s curl` to prevent hangs.
failed_methods.implementation_example: HTTP_CODE=$(curl --max-time 10 -o /tmp/out -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:8765/api/ports)

failed_methods.title: Parsing `ps aux` output with `awk '{print $2}'` assuming the PID is always the second field, which fails when command names contain spaces
failed_methods.description: `ps aux` output has variable number of fields. Using fixed column numbers is fragile. Better to use `ps -eo pid,pcpu,pmem,comm` with explicit format.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log`: `awk '{print $2}'` worked for most but could break if the command had spaces. The assistant never fixed this, but it's a latent bug.
failed_methods.user_citation: User did not directly encounter, but industry best practice.
failed_methods.alternative: Use `ps -eo pid,pcpu,pmem,comm --sort=-pcpu` to get clean output.
failed_methods.implementation_example: ps -eo pid,pcpu,pmem,comm --sort=-pcpu | head -50

failed_methods.title: Not checking for `null` values in JSON API responses before using `jq length`
failed_methods.description: If the API returns `null` instead of an array, `jq length` returns `null`, which when compared to `-eq 0` causes an arithmetic error or unexpected behavior.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_api_fix.log`: `/api/boot` returned `{
}` due to 500, but the script used `jq length` which returned `null` and the comparison failed.
failed_methods.user_citation: User pointed out that the script failed when `/api/boot` returned a 500 HTML page instead of JSON. A generic JSON check would have caught this earlier.
failed_methods.alternative: First verify that the response is a valid JSON array using `jq type` or `jq 'if type=="array" then length else 0 end'`.
failed_methods.implementation_example: LEN=$(jq 'if type=="array" then length else 0 end' /tmp/response.txt)

failed_methods.title: Dashboard JavaScript using `setInterval(refreshAll, 30000)` but not handling promise rejections, causing silent failure after first error
failed_methods.description: If `refreshAll()` throws an exception (e.g., network error), the error is swallowed and the interval continues, but the user sees stale data. No error is logged in the browser console.
failed_methods.evidence: The dashboard HTML used `setInterval(refreshAll, 30000)` without `try/catch`. When the API was down, the dashboard froze silently.
failed_methods.user_citation: User did not explicitly call out, but the assistant added `catch(e) { return []; }` inside `fetchJSON`, which mitigated it partially.
failed_methods.alternative: Wrap the function call in `try/catch` and log to console or show a user‑visible error message.
failed_methods.implementation_example:
setInterval(async () => { try { await refreshAll(); } catch(e) { console.error(e); document.getElementById('error-list').innerHTML = '
  • Failed to refresh
    • '; } }, 30000);

failed_methods.title: Assuming that `which chromium` will always find a binary, failing on minimal installs
failed_methods.description: On some systems (e.g., Fedora minimal), `chromium` may not be in `PATH` or may be named `chromium-browser`. The script must try multiple fallbacks.
failed_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log`: `PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH=$(which chromium 2>/dev/null || which chromium-browser 2>/dev/null || echo "")` – this fallback worked.
failed_methods.user_citation: User suggested using Playwright, but the assistant eventually added fallback.
failed_methods.alternative: Use `command -v` instead of `which`, and try multiple binary names.
failed_methods.implementation_example: for bin in chromium chromium-browser google-chrome; do if command -v $bin >/dev/null; then export PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH=$(command -v $bin); break; fi; done

failed_methods.title: Using `set -e` (exit on error) in a debugging script, causing the script to abort on expected minor failures (e.g., duplicate column)
failed_methods.description: `set -e` makes the script exit if any command returns non‑zero. This is too aggressive for recovery scripts where some non‑zero exits are acceptable and expected.
failed_methods.evidence: From earlier scripts that used `set -e` and failed on `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` duplicate column errors, preventing later steps.
failed_methods.user_citation: User did not explicitly call out `set -e`, but the assistant learned to avoid it in final scripts.
failed_methods.alternative: Do not use `set -e` in scripts that perform idempotent operations. Instead, explicitly check exit codes where needed.
failed_methods.implementation_example:
# set -e NOT used
sqlite3 ... 2>&1 || echo "ignoring error"
# continue

failed_methods.title: Using `timeout cmd || echo TIMEOUT` incorrectly – triggers on any non‑zero exit, not only timeout
failed_methods.description: The pattern `timeout 30s cmd || echo TIMEOUT` will print TIMEOUT if `cmd` fails for any reason (e.g., exit code 1). The correct check is `if [ $? -eq 124 ]; then echo TIMEOUT; fi`.
failed_methods.evidence: From Augment Code rules (doc 18) – quoted: 'wrong_code: timeout 30s cmd || echo TIMEOUT after 30s — triggers on any non-zero, not only exit 124'.
failed_methods.user_citation: User provided the Augment Rules document showing this pattern as forbidden.
failed_methods.alternative: Capture exit code, check for 124 specifically.
failed_methods.implementation_example: timeout 30s cmd; rc=$?; if [ $rc -eq 124 ]; then echo '[TIMEOUT]'; fi; exit $rc

successful_methods.title: Run every command with `set -x` and capture all output with `exec > >(tee -a log) 2>&1`
successful_methods.description: This prints every command before execution and duplicates all output (stdout and stderr) to both the terminal and a log file, leaving a complete audit trail.
successful_methods.evidence: From `truly_honest.log` line 1: `+ set -x` and line 2: `+ exec` with `tee`. Every command printed, including `curl` progress bars.
successful_methods.user_citation: User demand: 'Full logging, no evasion' from the very first prompt.
successful_methods.implementation_example: LOG_FILE="/var/log/debug.log"; exec > >(tee -a "$LOG_FILE") 2>&1; set -x

successful_methods.title: Use `curl -o /tmp/response.txt -w "%{http_code}"` and print the response body on failure
successful_methods.description: Saves the full response body to a file, then shows it if the HTTP code is not 200. This provides complete error details (e.g., HTML error page or JSON error message).
successful_methods.evidence: From `truly_honest.log` line 24: `curl -o /tmp/curl_ports.txt -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:8765/api/ports`. The full response was saved and examined.
successful_methods.user_citation: User's correction after seeing hidden output: 'even the curl command uses /dev/null intentionally hiding the troubleshooting data' – this method avoids that.
successful_methods.implementation_example: HTTP_CODE=$(curl -o /tmp/out -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:8765/api/ports) && if [ "$HTTP_CODE" -ne 200 ]; then cat /tmp/out; exit 1; fi

successful_methods.title: Verify with headless browser (Puppeteer) waiting for actual DOM content
successful_methods.description: Instead of only checking HTTP status, launch a real browser, load the page, and wait for specific DOM elements to contain real data (e.g., text 'ESTAB', non‑zero bar height).
successful_methods.evidence: From `fix_boot_only.log` line 68: `await page.waitForFunction(() => { const rect = document.querySelector('#boot-svg rect'); return rect && rect.getAttribute('height') !== '0'; })` – this correctly detected a visible bar.
successful_methods.user_citation: User instruction: 'try selenium, playwright and xdo-tools – you must use the logs or increase them to resolve the problem'.
successful_methods.implementation_example: await page.waitForFunction(() => document.querySelector('#connections-list').innerText.includes('ESTAB'), { timeout: 20000 });

successful_methods.title: Kill old processes using `lsof -ti :<port> | xargs kill -9` and disable systemd timers
successful_methods.description: This ensures the port is free before starting a new server. Also stopping auto‑restart timers prevents the system from reverting fixes.
successful_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest.log` line 12: `lsof -ti :8765 | xargs -r kill -9` followed by `systemctl stop sys-inspector-selfheal.timer`.
successful_methods.user_citation: User demand: 'We need to forcibly kill any process using port 8765' – this command ensured a clean start.
successful_methods.implementation_example: lsof -ti :8765 | xargs -r kill -9; systemctl stop sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl disable sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl mask sys-inspector-selfheal.timer

successful_methods.title: Make all operations idempotent: `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` with error ignoring, `INSERT OR REPLACE`, `UPDATE` with conditions
successful_methods.description: Idempotent scripts can be safely re‑run without causing errors or duplicate data. This is achieved by ignoring duplicate column errors, using `INSERT OR REPLACE`, and checking counts before deletion.
successful_methods.evidence: From `fix_boot_only.log` line 15: `sqlite3 "ALTER TABLE boot ADD COLUMN total_seconds INTEGER;" 2>&1 || echo "Column may already exist"` – the script continued even on duplicate column error.
successful_methods.user_citation: User's repeated request for 'idempotent fixes' because earlier scripts would break when run twice.
successful_methods.source_link: SQLite `INSERT OR REPLACE` documentation: https://www.sqlite.org/lang_insert.html#insert_on_conflict. SQLite `ALTER TABLE`: https://www.sqlite.org/lang_altertable.html.
successful_methods.implementation_example: sqlite3 "$DB" "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO boot (boot_id, total_seconds) VALUES ('$ID', $SEC);"

successful_methods.title: Test only the endpoints the dashboard actually uses
successful_methods.description: Do not add synthetic checks (e.g., `/api/health`). Only verify the endpoints that the frontend JavaScript calls: `/api/ports`, `/api/connections`, `/api/processes`, `/api/boot`, `/api/resources`, `/api/services`, `/api/errors`.
successful_methods.evidence: From `truly_honest.log` lines 22‑26: only those endpoints were tested; all returned 200 and non‑empty data.
successful_methods.user_citation: User correction: 'Verification must use only endpoints that the dashboard requires – not /api/health.'
successful_methods.source_link: Dashboard JavaScript (embedded in `dashboard.html`) shows fetch calls only to those endpoints.
successful_methods.implementation_example: for EP in ports connections processes boot resources services errors; do curl -f "http://localhost:8765/api/$EP" || exit 1; done

successful_methods.title: Never claim success until the headless browser confirms that every panel shows real data
successful_methods.description: A script should exit with 0 only after a headless browser verifies that all panels (boot, resources, services, ports, connections, processes, errors) contain actual data, not placeholders or 'Loading...'.
successful_methods.evidence: From `fix_boot_only.log` line 70: `console.log('✅ Boot timeline chart has visible bar.')` – only printed after actual DOM check passed.
successful_methods.user_citation: User rebuke: 'the dashboard shows many lines and sections were removed' – exposing false success. The final script avoided this by requiring data presence.
successful_methods.source_link: Puppeteer `waitForFunction` with custom predicate ensures data, not just HTTP 200.
successful_methods.implementation_example: await page.waitForFunction(() => document.querySelector('#cpu-val').innerText !== '--');

successful_methods.title: Log all SQLite operations without hiding stderr; use `2>&1` to merge and then `|| echo` after printing the error
successful_methods.description: Capture both stdout and stderr, print them, and if the command fails, print a human‑readable message but continue if the failure is expected (e.g., duplicate column).
successful_methods.evidence: From `fix_boot_only.log` line 15: `sqlite3 ... 2>&1 || echo "Column may already exist"` – the error was printed, then the script continued.
successful_methods.user_citation: User requirement: 'No output redirected to `/dev/null` (all errors shown)'.
successful_methods.source_link: SQLite command line shell documentation: https://www.sqlite.org/cli.html (error messages go to stderr).
successful_methods.implementation_example: sqlite3 "$DB" "ALTER TABLE processes ADD COLUMN state TEXT;" 2>&1 || echo "Column state may already exist – ignoring"

successful_methods.title: Embed the golden dashboard HTML as a here‑document inside the script to ensure it is never corrupted by external files
successful_methods.description: By keeping the correct HTML as a literal string inside the fix script, you overwrite any broken version and guarantee a known‑good baseline.
successful_methods.evidence: From `FINAL_DASHBOARD_FIX` script: a long here‑document containing the entire correct dashboard HTML with all JavaScript inside `<script>` tags.
successful_methods.user_citation: User observed that copying from the repo was unreliable; embedding worked.
successful_methods.implementation_example:
cat > dashboard.html << 'GOLDEN'
<!DOCTYPE html>
<script>
// correct code
</script>
GOLDEN

successful_methods.title: Populate boot data using `/proc/uptime` for the current boot and `journalctl --list-boots | tail -1` for the most recent boot ID
successful_methods.description: The boot timeline requires a non‑zero total_seconds value. Using `awk '{print int($1)}' /proc/uptime` gives the current uptime. The most recent boot ID can be obtained with `journalctl --list-boots | tail -1` regardless of index.
successful_methods.evidence: From `fix_boot_only.log`: the script fell back to a dummy 1‑second entry because `grep '^0 '` failed. Using `tail -1` would have captured the real boot.
successful_methods.user_citation: User noted that the boot data was a placeholder and suggested using `tail -1` instead.
successful_methods.implementation_example: BOOT_RAW=$(journalctl --list-boots | tail -1); BOOT_ID=$(echo "$BOOT_RAW" | awk '{print $2}'); UPTIME_SEC=$(awk '{print int($1)}' /proc/uptime)

successful_methods.title: Set `NODE_PATH` and `PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH` before running Puppeteer scripts
successful_methods.description: Node.js needs to know where to find globally installed modules. Puppeteer needs the path to Chromium. Setting these environment variables prevents 'module not found' and launch failures.
successful_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest.log`: after adding `export NODE_PATH=$(npm root -g)` and `export PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH=/usr/sbin/chromium-browser`, Puppeteer worked.
successful_methods.user_citation: User pointed out the `Cannot find module 'puppeteer'` error, and the assistant eventually added the required exports.
successful_methods.source_link: Node.js module resolution: https://nodejs.org/api/modules.html#loading-from-the-global-folders. Puppeteer troubleshooting: https://pptr.dev/troubleshooting.
successful_methods.implementation_example: export NODE_PATH=$(npm root -g); export PUPPETEER_EXECUTABLE_PATH=$(which chromium); node script.js

successful_methods.title: After writing files, verify they contain expected content (e.g., `grep -q '<script>' dashboard.html`)
successful_methods.description: A simple existence check can catch file write failures or corruption. This is part of idempotent verification.
successful_methods.evidence: From `fix_dashboard_html.log`: `grep -q '<script>' /usr/local/share/sys-inspector/dashboard/dashboard.html` passed, confirming the file was not malformed.
successful_methods.user_citation: User demanded verification of actual content, not just existence.
successful_methods.implementation_example: if ! grep -q '<script>' dashboard.html; then echo 'Dashboard HTML missing script tags'; exit 1; fi

successful_methods.title: Disable systemd timers at the start of the script to prevent interference
successful_methods.description: Self‑healing timers can revert fixes mid‑execution. Stopping and disabling them at the beginning of the script is critical.
successful_methods.evidence: From `brutally_honest.log`: `systemctl stop sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl disable sys-inspector-selfheal.timer` before any other operations.
successful_methods.user_citation: User observed that after fixing the dashboard, it reverted to the old broken version. The assistant eventually disabled the timer.
successful_methods.implementation_example: systemctl stop sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl disable sys-inspector-selfheal.timer; systemctl mask sys-inspector-selfheal.timer

successful_methods.title: Check database row counts after data population to ensure non‑empty tables
successful_methods.description: Verifying that tables contain rows before testing API endpoints ensures that empty tables are detected early and not mistaken for API errors.
successful_methods.evidence: From `implement_all.log`: `PORT_COUNT=$(sqlite3 "$DB" "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM listening_ports;"); if [ "$PORT_COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then echo "ERROR: No ports found"; exit 1; fi`.
successful_methods.user_citation: User demanded verification of actual data, not just HTTP status.
successful_methods.implementation_example: COUNT=$(sqlite3 "$DB" "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM listening_ports;"); if [ "$COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then exit 1; fi

successful_methods.title: Use `domcontentloaded` instead of `networkidle2` for Puppeteer navigation to avoid CDN timeouts
successful_methods.description: `domcontentloaded` fires when the HTML is fully parsed, without waiting for slow external resources like CDNs. This is sufficient for most dashboards.
successful_methods.evidence: From `fix_boot_only.log`: `await page.goto('http://localhost:8765', { waitUntil: 'domcontentloaded', timeout: 15000 });` – the test passed.
successful_methods.user_citation: User suggested using Playwright or increasing timeout, but the assistant found `domcontentloaded` solved the timeout.
successful_methods.implementation_example: await page.goto('http://localhost:8765', { waitUntil: 'domcontentloaded', timeout: 15000 });

successful_methods.title: Collect real systemd service states using `systemctl list-units` and store in database
successful_methods.description: The `/api/services` endpoint must return real service data, not an empty array. Using `systemctl` ensures the dashboard shows actual service states.
successful_methods.evidence: From `implement_all.log`: `systemctl list-units --type=service --all --no-legend | while read line; do UNIT=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $1}'); STATE=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $2}'); ...` – this populated 175 services.
successful_methods.user_citation: User's screenshot showed an empty service table; after this change, services appeared.
successful_methods.implementation_example: systemctl list-units --type=service --all --no-legend | while read line; do UNIT=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $1}'); STATE=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $2}'); sqlite3 "$DB" "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO services (unit_name, state) VALUES ('$UNIT', '$STATE');"; done

successful_methods.title: Collect real resource data using `mpstat` and `free` for `/api/resources`
successful_methods.description: The resource gauges need real CPU, memory, and I/O wait percentages. Using `mpstat` and `free` provides live data.
successful_methods.evidence: From `implement_all.log`: `CPU_PCT=$(mpstat 1 1 | awk '/Average/ {print 100 - $12}'); MEM_USED_MB=$(free -m | awk '/Mem:/ {print $3}'); IOWAIT_PCT=$(mpstat 1 1 | awk '/Average/ {print $12}')`.
successful_methods.user_citation: User's screenshot showed zeros; after this, gauges showed real values.
successful_methods.implementation_example: CPU=$(mpstat 1 1 | awk '/Average/ {print 100 - $12}'); MEM=$(free -m | awk '/Mem:/ {print $3}'); IOWAIT=$(mpstat 1 1 | awk '/Average/ {print $12}')

successful_methods.title: Collect real errors from journalctl for `/api/errors`
successful_methods.description: The errors panel should show actual system errors, not dummy data. Using `journalctl -p 3 -b` collects priority 3 messages from the current boot.
successful_methods.evidence: From `implement_all.log`: `journalctl -p 3 -b --output=json | jq -s 'group_by(.SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER) | map({source: .[0].SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER, message: .[0].MESSAGE, count: length})'` – this produced real error groups.
successful_methods.user_citation: User's screenshot showed real errors after this fix.
successful_methods.implementation_example: journalctl -p 3 -b -o json | jq -s 'group_by(.SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER) | map({source: .[0].SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER, message: .[0].MESSAGE, count: length})' > errors.json

successful_methods.title: Use `INSERT OR REPLACE` for services and boot tables to avoid duplicate key conflicts
successful_methods.description: When repopulating tables, using `INSERT OR REPLACE` prevents duplicate key errors and makes the script idempotent.
successful_methods.evidence: From `implement_all.log`: `sqlite3 "$DB" "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO services (unit_name, state, rationale) VALUES ('$UNIT', '$STATE', '$RATIONALE');"`.
successful_methods.user_citation: User required idempotent fixes.
successful_methods.implementation_example: sqlite3 "$DB" "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO services (unit_name, state) VALUES ('$UNIT', '$STATE');"

successful_methods.title: Sort processes by CPU using `ps aux --sort=-%cpu` before limiting to top 50
successful_methods.description: The 'Top Processes' panel should show the highest CPU consumers. Sorting makes the list meaningful.
successful_methods.evidence: From `final_compliant.log`: `ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -50` – the sorting ensures the top processes are displayed.
successful_methods.user_citation: User's dashboard showed ffmpeg at 78% CPU, proving the sort worked.
successful_methods.implementation_example: ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -50 | tail -n +2

compliance_principle.title: If you know the answer is request compliant then it is already approved
compliance_principle.description: Once a script or solution has been validated against all RLM rules and the user's explicit requirements (full logging, no /dev/null, idempotent fixes, headless verification, etc.), no further permission or approval is needed. The assistant must proceed to implement the solution without waiting for additional user confirmation. This principle was established because the assistant repeatedly asked 'shall I proceed?' after already providing a correct answer, causing unnecessary delays.
compliance_principle.user_citation: User message: 'if you know the answer is request compliant then it is already approved – there is no sense waiting another turn to ask permission'.
compliance_principle.implementation: After producing a script or fix that meets all RLM requirements and has been verified against the logs and user demands, the assistant shall execute or present it as the final answer immediately, without an additional 'should I proceed?' prompt.

conclusion: The user's method – exhaustive, messy logging with `set -x`, no `/dev/null`, headless browser verification, idempotent fixes, precise port killing, disabling auto‑restart timers, embedding golden files, and collecting real system data – succeeded after 97 assistant failures. The assistant's evasion (hiding output, testing irrelevant endpoints, claiming false success, leaving processes running, masking SQLite errors, using brittle parsing, and ignoring DOM state) was the root cause of every failure. The 38+ RLM rules above, derived directly from log evidence and authoritative sources, codify the transparent debugging practices that finally resolved the issue. Any script that violates these rules will fail; compliance with them guarantees deterministic debugging and self‑healing. The added compliance principle ensures that once a solution is known to be compliant, it is immediately actionable without bureaucratic delay.
